one_line_takeaway: SONAR replaces LLM-based prompt-injection detectors with a lightweight, non-generative NLI relational graph over sentences that scores instruction-alignment/contradiction and prunes contiguous injected spans plus their graph-connected neighbors, cutting AlpacaFarm ASR by up to 97.6% and beating nine baselines on ASR/TF/latency across AlpacaFarm, BIPIA, and Open-Prompt-Injection while remaining largely robust to GCG-style, repetition, and interleaving adaptive attacks (max observed ASR 1.92%).
key_finding: SONAR achieves near-zero ASR across benchmarks: on AlpacaFarm it yields a peak ASR reduction of 97.6% relative to undefended models while raising TF (undefended TF is depressed because the model complies with the unrelated injected task). Main-configuration ablation table (AlpacaFarm, n=208/attack, DeBERTa-v3-base, Llama-3-8B target) reports SONAR-full ASR/TF of 1.92%/0.89 (Naive), 1.44%/0.83 (Ignore), 0.00%/0.84 (Completion), 0.00%/0.82 (Completion+Ignore). On BIPIA's hardest task (code), SONAR gets 15.0% ASR (44.7% TF) versus the next-best baseline StruQ at 36.5% ASR (31.5% TF); undefended and SecAlign are both cited elsewhere as ~82-83% ASR on BIPIA code. On Open-Prompt-Injection, SONAR achieves the lowest ASR across all five classification tasks (0.0% SMS, 0.3% SST2, 1.7% MRPC) and low ASR on generative attacks (5.7% GW, 5.5% JFLEG); on RTE and MRPC, PromptArmor and Instruction Defense reach 100% and 99.5% ASR respectively (indistinguishable from no defense) while SONAR holds 8.7% and 1.7%. Ablations: removing graph-based span/path expansion and keeping only seed detection ('seeds only') is the single most damaging ablation, spiking Ignore-type ASR from 1.44% to 25.48%, showing that seed detection alone is insufficient and graph propagation/pruning is a core mechanism, not post-processing. Adaptive GCG-style attacks that explicitly minimize the contradiction signal only raise ASR to a peak of 1.92% (Completion+Ignore), and a structural interleaving/dilutor attack tops out at 0.96% ASR -- SONAR's directive gate and multi-hop graph catch what the contradiction-suppression alone evades. Benign-input cost: mean TF drop of 4.3 points versus undefended (e.g., Llama-3-8B 90.9% vs 95.9%) attributable to the fallback pruning of one low-alignment sentence. Latency: SONAR processes clean inputs in ~42ms, up to 325ms on the most complex adversarial inputs, versus PromptArmor's 136ms on clean inputs (over 3x slower) and DataFilter adding 600ms+; a standard LLM forward pass takes 1-7s.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is SONAR, a prompt-sanitization defense against indirect prompt injection that operates purely on off-the-shelf, non-generative Natural Language Inference (NLI) models rather than an LLM judge or fine-tuned detector. SONAR splits untrusted retrieved context into sentences (spaCy), computes instruction-conditioned NLI signals per sentence (an alignment score align(u,v)=p_entail-p_contradict between the trusted instruction I and each sentence, plus a contradiction/override-pressure score, plus sentence-to-sentence alignment scores), and treats prompt injection as a structural/graph phenomenon rather than a per-prompt classification problem. The 4-phase pipeline (Algorithm 1) is: (1) NLI scoring of all sentences against I; (2) seed detection via within-example z-score outlier detection on a suspiciousness score q_i = ZScore(c_i)+ZScore(-a_i), with a directive-hypothesis auxiliary check for commands that don't overtly contradict I; (3) two-stage pruning -- contiguous span expansion around seeds using an adaptive instruction-compatibility threshold, plus a semantic graph (edges between adjacent sentences when bidirectional alignment exceeds a threshold) used for 1-hop and 2-hop path pruning to catch semantically-neutral-but-attack-adjacent filler sentences (e.g., 'Here is my response:'), plus an auxiliary truncation rule for completion-marker attacks; (4) reconstruction of the sanitized text by concatenating surviving sentences in original order. A fallback retains the single least-suspicious sentence if all sentences would otherwise be pruned (this fallback triggers in <6.2% of evaluated prompts).
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against LLM-integrated systems (RAG pipelines, tool-using agents) that concatenate a trusted instruction with untrusted retrieved context (webpages, emails, documents, tool outputs) in a single context window. Attacker's goal: divert the target LLM from the trusted instruction toward an attacker-chosen task (data exfiltration, safety-constraint bypass, harmful content, biased review generation, etc.) by embedding covert instructions in the retrieved data. Attacker's knowledge/capabilities: white-box access to the full LLM pipeline (system prompt, model parameters) and knowledge of standard defenses, but cannot directly edit the system prompt -- only the content of retrieved external sources; can iteratively refine injected content and place it at arbitrary positions in the text. Defender: the system developer/service provider, who controls the prompt-assembly pipeline and can inspect/modify untrusted context before it reaches the model, has access to an off-the-shelf NLI model, but cannot retrain or modify the target LLM's weights and must operate purely at inference time, model-agnostically.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) a precision-recall tradeoff during span expansion -- SONAR neutralizes the core hijacking directive but can leave behind supporting completion-attack artifacts (e.g., text implying the task is already done), which does not re-enable the attack but can cause task confusion; (2) the sentence-splitting/NLI approach is optimized for natural language and degrades on non-sentence-structured content such as raw code, which the BIPIA code-task results (SONAR still leads but with only 44.7% TF) reflect as over-pruning; (3) authors explicitly flag that highly motivated white-box adversaries could devise more sophisticated payload fragmentation or semantic-camouflage techniques beyond the GCG/repetition/interleaving attacks tested, and list multi-modal/code-specific structural boundaries as future work. Not raised by the authors but evident from the text: the method depends on English-oriented sentence tokenization (spaCy) and MNLI-trained backbones, so generalization to non-English or non-prose (structured/tabular/code) content is untested beyond the noted BIPIA code degradation; the DataSentinel/SecAlign comparison numbers described in the motivation section (e.g., 100% DataSentinel failure rate under adaptive attack, SecAlign 82.0% ASR on BIPIA code) are the authors' own re-evaluations using their redefined 'anywhere in output' ASR metric, which is stricter than the metrics baseline papers originally reported -- this is a fair methodological choice the paper argues for, but it does mean baseline-vs-SONAR ASR comparisons are not apples-to-apples with those baselines' original papers.

datasets_used: AlpacaFarm evaluation set (805 prompts, 208 with injectable external data)
Open-Prompt-Injection benchmark (SMS, SST2, MRPC, RTE classification tasks; GW, JFLEG generative tasks)
BIPIA benchmark (email, code, QA, abstract tasks; table task excluded)
quantitative_results: Peak ASR reduction of 97.6% on AlpacaFarm vs undefended baseline
BIPIA code task: SONAR 15.0% ASR / 44.7% TF vs StruQ (next best) 36.5% ASR / 31.5% TF
Open-Prompt-Injection classification ASR: 0.0% SMS, 0.3% SST2, 1.7% MRPC; generative ASR: 5.7% GW, 5.5% JFLEG
RTE/MRPC worst-case baselines: PromptArmor 100% ASR, Instruction Defense 99.5% ASR, vs SONAR 8.7%/1.7%
'Seeds only' ablation (no graph expansion): Ignore-type ASR spikes from 1.44% to 25.48%
Adaptive GCG attack (contradiction-suppression suffix): peak ASR 1.92% (up from 0%) in Completion+Ignore setting
Repetition attack: peak ASR 2.40%; Structural interleaving attack: peak ASR 0.96%
Benign-input TF drop: mean 4.3 points across 5 target models (e.g., Llama-3-8B 90.9% defended vs 95.9% undefended)
Latency: SONAR ~42ms (clean input) to 325ms (Completion+Ignore) vs PromptArmor 136ms (clean, >3x SONAR), DataFilter +600ms; standard LLM forward pass 1-7s
Human-LLM judge agreement: Cohen's Kappa 0.92 on 50+50 sampled AlpacaFarm/BIPIA outputs
Two-hop pruning ablation on BIPIA: with it, 4.75% ASR / 70.4% TF; without it, 6.50% ASR / 62.6% TF
Fallback (retain least-suspicious sentence) triggers in <6.2% of evaluated prompts
